cataloging_subject.label: The Lyf of the Noble and Crysten Prynce, Charles the Grete
cataloging_subject.scope_note: William Caxton's three-book Middle English prose translation and adaptation of Jean Bagnyon's French Histoire de Charlemagne, completed 18 June 1485 and first printed by Caxton at Westminster on 1 December 1485. The cataloged work includes Caxton's prologue, a chapter table, eighty-six numbered chapters, the source author's excuse and envoy within Book III, and Caxton's final explicit. It is distinct from Bagnyon's abstract French work and Caxton's unidentified printed exemplar; the surviving French manuscripts and prints; British Library C.10.b.9 and its fragments; Sidney J. H. Herrtage's 1880-1881 edition and 1967 reprint; the University of Michigan encoding; and the repository XML artifact.

form_statements.description: Caxton's prologue, table, three-book narrative, source-author envoy, and closer are prose. Ten L elements in the repository XML occur in Herrtage's modern front matter or quoted/editorial material and do not make the cataloged work mixed-form.

genre_statements.label: Chronicle romance
notes: The compilation presents legendary dynastic and sacred history through romance narrative; the label does not assert modern historical reliability.

genre_statements.label: Crusade romance
notes: Scoped to the Holy Land expedition and the Fierabras/relic-conflict material rather than treated as the sole genre of the whole compilation.

subject_statements.label: Christian-Saracen conflict and conversion
notes: The label describes the romance's polemical representations, warfare, and conversion episodes; it does not endorse them or identify fictional categories with modern peoples.

subject_statements.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy

summaries.value: Caxton's three-book prose work gathers the legendary life and campaigns of Charlemagne. Book I traces the Christianization and succession of the kings of France, Charlemagne's upbringing and rule, and his expedition to the Holy Land. Book II recounts Oliver's combat with Fierabras, Fierabras's conversion, the captivity and rescue of the Twelve Peers, Floripas's aid, the recovery of relics, and the defeat and conversion of Balan's forces. Book III follows Charlemagne's Spanish conquests, the Camino and Galician campaigns, the treason of Ganelon, the deaths of Roland and Oliver at Roncevaux, Charlemagne's final acts and death, and the source author's summary and envoy.

The work has Caxton's prologue on William Daubeny's request, a prospective table, and eighty-six numbered prose chapters arranged in three books and eight parts: Book I has 5 + 5 + 3 chapters; Book II has 16 + 17 + 16; Book III has 14 + 10. Book III's final two numbered chapters are the French source author's excuse and envoy, followed by Caxton's unnumbered explicit. The XML therefore has eighty-seven DIV3 containers: eighty-six numbered chapters plus the final closer.

The XML header's first label, 'Fierabras. English (Middle English) Prose.', describes only the best-known narrative component and is not an adequate title or boundary for this three-book compilation. Jean Bagnyon's French work is untitled in its manuscripts and is now conventionally called Histoire de Charlemagne. Caxton's exact printed French exemplar is unresolved among five candidates; Herrtage's use of the 1483 British Library print for comparison must not be converted into a direct-source claim.

extent.description: The intrinsic structure has a Caxton prologue, table, three books containing eighty-six numbered prose chapters, and an unnumbered closer. The XML BODY has six DIV1, eight DIV2, and eighty-seven DIV3 elements because the closer is encoded as an additional DIV3. Its ten L elements belong to Herrtage's introduction or embedded/editorial quotation and are not evidence that Caxton's prose work is mixed-form.

content_parts.label: Caxton's prologue
content_parts.summary: Caxton explains that William Daubeny asked him to translate the French book, commends the histories of virtuous rulers, and introduces Charlemagne.

content_parts.label: Table of chapters
content_parts.summary: A prospective table organizes all eighty-six numbered chapters under three books and eight parts.

content_parts.label: Book I
content_parts.summary: Thirteen chapters in three parts recount the early French kings and Clovis's conversion, Pepin and Charlemagne's life and governance, and Charlemagne's Holy Land expedition and relics.

content_parts.label: Book I, Part I
content_parts.summary: Five chapters on Clodoneus, Clotildis, Clovis's conversion and victories, and his baptism by Saint Remigius.

content_parts.label: Book I, Part II
content_parts.summary: Five chapters on Pepin's accession and Charlemagne's body, household, education, learning, governance, and charity.

content_parts.label: Book I, Part III
content_parts.summary: Three chapters on the patriarch of Jerusalem's appeal, Charlemagne's expedition to the Holy Land, and the relics he brings from Constantinople and Jerusalem.

content_parts.label: Book II
content_parts.summary: Forty-nine chapters in three parts narrate Oliver's combat with Fierabras, the captivity and rescue of the peers, Floripas's aid and the recovery of relics, and Charlemagne's campaign against Balan.

content_parts.label: Book II, Part I
content_parts.summary: Sixteen chapters centered on Oliver's single combat with Fierabras, Fierabras's defeat and conversion, and Oliver's capture.

content_parts.label: Book II, Part II
content_parts.summary: Seventeen chapters on the peers' imprisonment, Floripas's intervention, Guy's embassy and love, and the defense of Aigremore.

content_parts.label: Book II, Part III
content_parts.summary: Sixteen chapters on Charlemagne's crossing at Mantrible, battles against Balan's forces, the recovery of relics, the conversions and marriages, and the return to France.

content_parts.label: Book III
content_parts.summary: Twenty-four chapters in two parts recount Charlemagne's Spanish and Galician campaigns, the Roncevaux betrayal and deaths, Charlemagne's final acts and death, and the source author's retrospective excuse and envoy.

content_parts.label: Book III, Part I
content_parts.summary: Fourteen chapters on Saint James's command, Charlemagne's Spanish and Galician conquests, churches, wonders, champions, and campaigns.

content_parts.label: Book III, Part II
content_parts.summary: Ten chapters on Ganelon's treason, Roncevaux, the deaths of Roland and Oliver, Ganelon's punishment, Charlemagne's laws and death, followed by the source author's excuse and envoy.

content_parts.label: Caxton's closer
content_parts.summary: The unnumbered final explicit identifies William Caxton after the source author's numbered envoy and the exact translation and printing dates.

locators.value: final DIV3 and TRAILER in DIV1 CharlesG:6

related_works.title: Histoire de Charlemagne (Jean Bagnyon's untitled-in-manuscript three-book French prose compilation)
notes: Caxton translated an unidentified physical copy of this work. Histoire de Charlemagne is a conventional title, not an authorial manuscript title; the 1478/1483 prints and the two surviving manuscripts are related carriers, not automatically Caxton's exemplar.

related_works.title: Speculum historiale
notes: Vincent de Beauvais's work is a mediated source used by Bagnyon, especially for Books I and III; it is not Caxton's direct physical copytext.

related_works.title: Fierabras (Old French chanson de geste and prose tradition)
notes: Bagnyon's Book II adapts the Fierabras tradition. The relation is mediated through Bagnyon's compilation, so the whole Caxton work should not be collapsed to Fierabras alone.

related_works.title: Historia Karoli Magni et Rotholandi (Pseudo-Turpin Chronicle)
notes: A mediated source tradition for Bagnyon's Charlemagne and Roncevaux material in Book III.

evidence.citation: Elisabeth de Bruijn, 'Catering to Different Tastes: The Reception of the French Story of Fierabras in England, the Low Countries, and Italy' (2023).
evidence.summary: Supports a printed French exemplar for Caxton while preserving uncertainty among five candidate early editions; the 1483 Herrtage comparison cannot be promoted to Caxton's source.

evidence.citation: e-codices, Fondation Martin Bodmer Cod. Bodmer 15.
evidence.summary: Identifies Bagnyon and one of the two manuscript witnesses, dates and describes the codex, and maps the complete three-book work on fols. 1r-117v.

evidence.citation: Bibliothèque de Genève, Catalogue des manuscrits français 1-198 (2011), Ms. fr. 188.
evidence.summary: Identifies Bagnyon, dates and describes BGE Ms. fr. 188, maps its three parts, and documents the two-manuscript tradition and source relationships.

Supplies stable entity IDs and access, rights, relation, and evidence distinctions for the repository artifact, U-M encoding, Herrtage editions, Caxton print and exemplar, Bagnyon work, French prints, and manuscripts.

open_questions.question: Which of the five candidate early printed editions of Bagnyon's work did Caxton translate, and can his physical copy be identified?
next_steps: Consult Hans-Erich Keller's 1992 critical edition, Lotte Hellinga's source analysis, and later stemmatic and provenance scholarship.
Keep the abstract French work, the unidentified physical exemplar, and every surviving print/manuscript carrier distinct.

open_questions.question: What evidence and scholarly sequence led from Herrtage's anonymous French-author description to the modern Jean Bagnyon attribution?
next_steps: Consult Keller's introduction and Bagnyon attribution history.
Preserve Herrtage's historical statement as edition evidence without treating it as the current preferred attribution.

open_questions.question: What issue-specific changes or new matter distinguish the 1967 one-volume reprint from the 1880-1881 edition beyond binding and title matter?
next_steps: Collate a complete 1967 physical or digital copy against the verified complete 1880-1881 scan.
Do not call the reprint photographic or reset without issue-level evidence.

open_questions.question: How should U-M's current public-domain statement, the embedded 1999 restrictive availability statement, and the CME root Unlicense be reconciled for redistribution of the encoding?
next_steps: Request clarification from U-M and Kyvero Press.
Keep copyright, historical access terms, and repository-license scope as separate assertions.

notes: The XML header's first title, 'Fierabras. English (Middle English) Prose.', is a legacy classification label that narrows a three-book Charlemagne compilation to one source component; it is not used as the preferred work title.

The XML's LANGUSAGE lists Latin and French but omits primary Middle English. French comparison passages and much Latin belong to Herrtage's editorial carrier; only brief Latin quotations are recorded as intrinsic quoted language.

The XML metrics—six DIV1, eight DIV2, eighty-seven DIV3, 262 PB, 376 MILESTONE, 337 NOTE1, and ten L—describe the encoding and edition. Intrinsic work extent is eighty-six numbered prose chapters plus prologue, table, and closer.

Rights and access conditions for the fifteenth-century work, physical prints and manuscripts, Herrtage editions, U-M encoding, provider images, microforms, and repository artifact remain attached to their separate lineage layers.
